title: Steam helped the thief steal my in-game items
review:
Every single time I log into my Steam account or sell/transfer in-game items, I’m forced to confirm the action through the Steam Mobile app — a security tool Valve swears will keep my account and hard-earned digital property safe.
But at 3:16 AM on March 18, while I was fast asleep, a thief broke into my account with zero push notifications, zero required confirmations from my mobile app, and zero effective oversight from Valve. They proceeded to steal 8 of my most valuable in-game items, worth a total of 400 USD, and sent them straight to their own account.
I gathered every piece of evidence of this theft and submitted it to Valve, only for them to brush me off completely. They claimed they were not involved, and that there was nothing they could do to fix the situation.
I’ve spent over 5000 USD on this platform. I trusted Valve’s promise that their mobile authenticator would protect my assets. Instead, Valve’s broken security system let a robber straight into my account, and now they’re washing their hands of the whole incident — leaving me stuck with the full 400 USD loss, a loss that never would have happened if their advertised security features actually worked as promised.

title: Worst customer support I have ever dealt with
review:
Finally about to use Steam for the first time. Usually just downloaded free games, but I decided to treat myself and get Helldivers 2. Enjoyed it for like five hours total over the course of a week. I don’t have tons of time to game. Then the devs(this is when the game first came out) released an update and I literally couldn’t play the game. Black screen after black screen.

I decided to troubleshoot. I troubleshooted to the point that my last and final option was to completely factory reset my operating system. I was not going to do that for a $39.99 game. I asked for a refund and Steam told me no. Why? Because even though I wasn’t actually playing the game the time I spent relaunching while trouble shooting still counted towards my playtime. So at this point I’m still unable to play and I’m stuck with a game I can’t play. After going back and forth they would not budge.

Month later the game finally works, but during that time as any responsible consumer would I initiated a claim through my bank. I didn’t even want the game anymore. I didn’t play it I just thought I’d test it. Then after the claim when I went to see if the game was still there and was still working to consider my options on how to proceed with Steam they revoked my license and restricted my payment claiming this is their policy to prevent fraud. They requested I cancel the claim and then I can get the license back.

I called my bank, cancelled the claim, sent them the letter that was provided by my bank stating the claim was cancelled and that $39.99 was deducted my account. Was that the end?

Nope. They wanted me to get an additional letter from my bank with their letter head to show further proof that the funds were transferred to them. At this point I’ve the threatened to call the FTC, CFPB, make a statement on the BBB and contact the Washington state attorney general because that is where Valve has their HQ. To go through all of this that could’ve been resolved quickly is insane.

Also live service games should not be subject to the 8 hours refund policy. 8 hours of a single player game is a good chunk of a game. Live service? That barely scratches the surface considered the intention behind the game is for it to be played for hundreds, even possibly over a thousand hours considering loyalty and if the live service has a long lifespan. Steam is awesome until you actually need assistance. You have been warned.

title: I'm happy with steam.
review:
I see a lot of negative experiences, some about support for older windows versions, some about negative customer support experiences.
It's always sad to see people having bad experiences.
However, I have absolutely nothing negative to say about Steam.
I've been on steam since April 2010 and have, at the time of this review, 222 games and 687 DLC's in my account.
In that time i've had bad purchases, payment errors, game services going offline, games no longer working, newly bought games not working and steamkeys not working. Customer support has always been able to help me. Either with solutions to make something work again, links to support from game developers, and in some cases where nothing worked they gave me refunds.
Steam itself is easy to use, has a HUGE amount of available games (including free), fun events and frequent discounts.
The steam community, aside from some toxic people, is mostly helpful and friendly. Offensive behaviour is punished and good behavior is rewarded with badges and rewards from other people.
There's a huge amount of collectibles from games and funny extra's can be bought with points, earned with purchases.

Overall, i'm happy with steam, more so then with other platforms, and will continue to use it in the future.
I can also recommend Steam to every other PC user, as it's safe, easy to use, beginner friendly and has an awesome support.

title: Very poor account security.
review: After lodging a complaint through Steam customer service and receiving an abusive response from one of their customer service agents, I attempted to escalate to somebody higher up. I then laid a complaint about that agent and left it at that. Around a month later my Steam login stopped working and I discovered my entire account (along with a decades-old games collection) had been deleted (not closed, not suspended... deleted). I requested help from Steam support who told me a request had come through for account deletion despite the fact that I didn't request anything of the sort. I suspect the deletion was queued by the agent I complained about, but naturally I couldn't prove it and none of the agents I corresponded with could confirm or deny. Even after escalating further I was told the account was gone and couldn't be recovered. All I received was an apology for 'the inconvenience'). I can't trust Valve or Steam ever again with my account information if its that simple for an account to be deleted without my security information being requested and confirmed by me. You'd think a reputable company would have taken such an incident more seriously.

title: I accidentally made an incorrect…
review:
I accidentally made an incorrect purchase on my account and contacted Steam twice to resolve the issue. It took a total of two days to receive a response. I was told the purchase could not be refunded if the in-game credits had been consumed—however, they were not used in any way.

I followed up a second time specifically to confirm that the credits had not been consumed, yet I received the same generic response: “In-game item purchases are not refundable if any items in the purchase have been consumed, modified, or transferred.”

Due to the lack of meaningful review of the case or support for an obvious accidental purchase, I will no longer be making purchases through this store or with this company.

I would strongly suggest that if reading this, care at all about how a company treats its customers, you consider doing the same.

title: I tried to buy Battlefield 6
review:
I tried to buy Battlefield 6. I have purchased games many times before on Steam, but this time the purchase failed. I tried again, but it failed once more. I waited a day and tried with my other card, but it still failed. At that point, the offer had already expired, but I received an email stating that I could still complete the purchase within the next 72 hours.
I tried to buy it three more times during those 72 hours, but it failed every single time. In total, I think I’ve tried to buy this game 12 times using 3 different cards. Therefore, I contacted support, but as usual, Steam is pathetic when it comes to customer service. It’s like they don’t even read my message.
They wrote that they needed screenshots of the purchase process, but I don’t know what they want pictures of. I confirmed the payment via my digital ID (MitID) and did everything I could. I have shopped with them many times before and entered everything correctly, so I don't see what I’m supposed to document. When I asked them what exactly I should send, they didn’t even give a proper answer. They just wrote that if I couldn’t provide those pictures, they would close the case.
I have had problems with them several times. Last time, my account was hacked, and I lost Counter-Strike skins worth thousands of dollars. Back then, they just told me it was my own problem. They never improve. I am done shopping on this platform. I have tried to buy a game 12 times without getting it, and now I can’t even get it on sale anymore I sincerely regret waiting so long to try to buy it on this platform. I should have just bought it on EA and I would have gotten the game on sale.

title: Great Platform, Frustrating Support - A Solid Three Stars
review:
Steam is, without a doubt, the go-to platform for PC gaming with its massive library, frequent sales, and solid community features. For that core service, it deserves recognition. However, my recent experience with their customer support has left me feeling stuck in a frustrating middle ground, forcing a three-star review.

The issue arose with a gift purchase. I bought a game for a family member, following the same process I've used many times before, only to encounter a technical hiccup where the game didn't share to their library as expected. Understanding that policies exist, I contacted support for a simple transfer or refund so I could repurchase it correctly—the game had zero minutes of playtime.

The response was a rigid, automated adherence to the 14-day refund policy, with no apparent consideration for the context: a legitimate gift, unplayed, and a customer just seeking to correct a simple error. The lack of human discretion or willingness to find a solution for a loyal user was profoundly disappointing. It felt like communicating with a system, not a support team.

This experience casts a shadow on the platform. While I'll continue to use Steam for its unbeatable game selection and deals, I now do so with caution. I’ve started considering alternative storefronts for gifts or larger purchases, wary of facing a similar brick wall if something goes slightly outside the standard script.

In summary: Five stars for the platform, one star for inflexible support. Averaged out to three. It’s a fantastic service until you need help, and then it becomes a significant source of frustration. They have the foundation to be perfect but need to reintroduce humanity into their customer service.

title: Steam Wallet Gifting Issue
review:
Steam Wallet Gifting Issue – My Full Experience with shitty steam support

I bought official Steam Wallet codes from a verified seller and withdraw the balance on my account. My only intention was to use that balance to gift a specific game to a friend. Both of us are in the same country, same region, same currency. There should be no problem.

But Steam blocks the purchase completely and says I cannot use my wallet balance to gift the game. They don’t warn users before adding funds, they don’t mention this restriction on the product page, and they still show the “Gift” option and “Wallet” payment option even though the transaction will fail every time.

When I contacted Steam Support, they told me this restriction cannot be removed and that I can only use my wallet funds to buy games for myself. That makes the balance useless for the purpose I added it for. After that, they closed my support ticket without offering any real solution.

This system is misleading, confusing, and unfair to customers who add wallet money specifically to gift games.

title: Archaic, Discriminatory Age Verification Policy & Robotic Support
review:
I am writing this review to highlight Valve/Steam's discriminatory and archaic age verification process, and the utterly useless, robotic support provided when attempting to resolve the issue.
I am a 40-year-old adult, yet I am restricted from fully accessing content I legally purchased on my account because their age verification system only accepts a credit card. This is utterly out of touch and discriminatory, operating under the assumption that every adult is entitled to or able to obtain a credit card. Other major platforms like Roblox use secure, modern methods (like Persona or photo ID verification), but Steam demands this singular, restrictive, and financial requirement.
Crucially, this method is also completely insecure and ineffective for age verification. The system only checks that the credit card is valid; it does not verify that the person using the card is the owner. A minor can easily bypass the age restriction by simply using a parent’s card, proving that Steam's policy fails on the grounds of both fairness and security.
Because of this flawed policy, my access to purchased games is restricted, rendering my investment useless. I contacted customer support, explaining that since they are preventing me from accessing the content I paid for, I required a refund.
The response was infuriating. I received nothing but generic, dictated policy replies—the kind of canned response you get from an uncaring AI drone, not a human being. They refused to treat my situation as an exceptional circumstance or acknowledge the discriminatory nature of their own policy.
My interaction has left me with the firm conclusion that Valve's reliance on this flawed, singular credit card verification method is purely due to organizational laziness and incompetence. They are unwilling to invest the resources to integrate secure, modern, and non-discriminatory verification options, prioritizing their own convenience over providing a fair and accessible platform for all paying adult customers

title: Hello Steam Support,
review:
Hello Steam Support,

I’m writing as a friend of the owner of ticket HT-F4B7-K8YW-44H2, because I personally witnessed everything that happened and I’m extremely disappointed with how this situation was handled.

My friend’s Steam account was hacked, and his items were stolen. He contacted support immediately, provided full evidence, timestamps, trade history — everything that could possibly be needed for a proper investigation. The stolen items were moved just a short time before he submitted the ticket, which makes the situation even more clear and traceable.

Yet all he received were generic, copy-paste responses and a refusal to help.
The explanation that “restoring stolen items affects the market” sounds like an excuse, not a real reason. Steam fully controls the trading system, item circulation, and account security — and still offers absolutely no protection when a user becomes a victim of a hack.

It’s frustrating to watch how a loyal user who followed all security rules, used Steam Guard, and did everything correctly is left without any support, compensation, or even a meaningful explanation. It genuinely feels like the case was not reviewed at all.

As someone who values the Steam platform, it’s painful to see how such a large company refuses to assist in a situation where help is clearly possible. I kindly ask you to reconsider this case and give it a proper, human review rather than an automated response.

Users deserve better.

Sincerely,
A concerned friend of the affected account owner

Steam Support has been one of the worst customer service experiences I’ve ever had.

My account was hacked and my items were stolen. I contacted support immediately, provided clear evidence, timestamps, and the full trade history. The items had been transferred less than a couple of days before I reported the issue — yet Steam refused to help in any meaningful way.

This is my Steam Support ticket: HT-F4B7-K8YW-44H2.

Instead of actually investigating my case, I received generic copy-paste responses. Steam claims they “cannot restore stolen items,” but this is nothing more than an internal policy choice, not a technical limitation. Users are completely unprotected, even when the entire trading and security system is controlled by Steam.

I followed every security requirement, used Steam Guard, and did everything correctly — yet I was left with zero protection, zero compensation, and zero accountability from Steam.

For a company of this size, this level of support is absolutely unacceptable.

If Steam truly cares about its users, my case should be reviewed again, with a proper explanation and actual effort — not automated replies.

Extremely disappointed.

title: One alternative to zoning hundreds of thousands of back-alley internet cafés
review:
I've used Steam on & off since around 2007. Although there was that time I lost upwards of fifty games due to an account deletion, I've restored most of my library since then (sort of, because you don't technically own the games when you buy them on Steam - you're purchasing a license - even though you may end up paying the same amount as you would for a physical copy). It isn't the only client I use. There are certain titles you can only find on Good Old Games, or Epic, or whatever EA is calling itself these days, but Steam has, in my opinion, the best real-time view of the video game consumer landscape short of subscribing to a gaming magazine. Their sales, demos, & bundles are abundant to say the least, besides a seemingly endless supply of free-to-play.

If you ever need support, however, I recommend accepting that they will not be able to help in most cases. The agents I've dealt with tend to be combative, willfully incompetent, & openly spiteful - which sort of comes with the territory, if you consider the platform's origins in attempting to manage one of the most notoriously hacked & cheated multiplayer games ever. On that front, huge success. The advances they've made to address issues related to latency are revolutionary in ways we probably won't fully come to appreciate for a long time. When you also consider how mindbogglingly global the community is, it's a wonder that I've only been targeted by technically gifted pranksters a handful of times.

Overall, if you're trying to avoid trolls, you're on your own when it comes to Steam. They're basically a federally protected sanctuary. That still hasn't deterred me from making it the primary client for organizing & accessing a majority of my favorite games. (UPDATE: it happened. support is just as liable to target users. I recommend steering clear from Steam, final answer.) There are also benefits to the overlay if you can get comfortable taking screenshots & recording your gameplay, as well as for troubleshooting, whether you elect to use their support or go straight to the developers.

When it comes to the buying part, no issues whatsoever. I've even been granted refunds for defective games long after the stated window, which is helpful since I'm about five (hundred) years behind on my reading list. Gamers (or anyone who shares a computer with one) will also find plenty of leisure bonuses in Wallpaper Engine, a free service with a massive collection of user-generated, interactive & customizable wallpapers, plus plenty of idle or desktop-style games that enable you to keep a responsive critter on your taskbar or even manage a miniature farm while you're supposed to be working.

There's also much to be said about their support for VR, which has been mostly outpacing Meta in terms of desktop interface - even though Valve hasn't managed to make their own hardware nearly as affordable yet. I can't comment on the Steam Deck, but it certainly seems popular enough from what I've read. In my humblest, wrestling with the abyss of filesystems & treading the Cyrillic backwaters of the internet for an obscure patch at four in the morning is central to the PC gaming experience, but maybe I'm old school that way.

9/10.

title: Steam Should Not Enable War-Linked Developers
review:
Steam’s global reach comes with global responsibility. It’s troubling to see games like Atomic Heart, Pathfinder, Rogue Trader and Warhammer 40,000: Space Marine 2 featured on the platform—titles developed by studios based in russia or with strong financial ties to the russian federation.

russia is currently waging a full-scale war against Ukraine and has a history of occupying Chechnya and parts of Georgia. These developers continue to pay taxes to a regime officially designated by multiple governments as a state sponsor of terrorism. By hosting and promoting these games, Steam is indirectly supporting the funding of a war machine responsible for immense human suffering.

Steam has the power to shape industry standards. Supporting humanitarian values means refusing to profit from entities that contribute to violence and oppression. I urge Valve to take a principled stand and reevaluate its partnerships with studios linked to authoritarian regimes.

title: Heartless Policy and No Compassion from Steam
review:
After losing his father, my son tried to access a shared Steam account that holds all the games he personally purchased. The account was originally in his father's name, and despite providing context (we have the log in details, but cannot authenticate as the mobile number has been deactivated), Steam flatly refused to allow access or transfer the games to a new account, there was no offer any meaningful solution. Their response was cold and bureaucratic, showing zero empathy for a grieving child trying to access content he paid for.

It’s deeply disappointing that a company as large and influential as Steam Powered would hide behind rigid policies instead of helping a loyal customer in a time of need. These games represent years of memories and investment, and to deny access simply because the account holder passed away is not just unfair—it’s cruel.

Shame on Steam!

title: After learning about the cancer patient…
review:
After learning about the cancer patient who downloaded a game from steam that resulted in him being hacked and his medical fund stolen, as a steam user I decided to look more into the user agreement as I thought it was a safe space like Apple App Store - I was shocked to find that steam holds no accountability or liability for any games that they host and promote as games leading to any damages and lost of data. This also included malware being attached to any games you may download. So not only do you not own any of the games you buy at retail price, you also run the risk of downloading malicious software that steam believe they have no responsibility in preventing or compensating.

I for one will not be using steam no longer for downloading any games as I don’t believe I should be held responsible for any misleading sales steam host and promote for sale.

I believe how steam has treated another user would also reflect how they treat me in that situation. My review is solely based on the stance that steam has taken over this and I believe it affects us all and is cause for concern.

I think this needs to be presented to the European Union or ofcom as I personally do not believe that this follows consumer protection laws and/or the online safety acts that aims to prevent the sale of malicious software by requiring online platforms to take steps to protect users from illegal content and criminal activity on their platforms. Making a user agreement that mitigate all responsibility is in clear defiance of this law imo.

title: Locked out of my account - customer service won't help
review:
I started using Steam and later on purchased a whole bunch of games under it a few years ago. At one point, I lost access to the e-mail address that I used as log-in e-mail address. Mind you, it was not a mistake on my part and I need not e.g. lose my Steam log-in password. So, I had the reasonable expectation that I would still be able to log into the Steam account and e.g. update the e-mail address. However, when I try to do so, Steam is insisting on sending a verification/security code to the registered e-mail address, which obviously wouldn't work. I never asked for this additional security feature and it's now keeping me from logging in and using the account (even from logging into the Steam client and uninstalling the locally installed games to free up hard drive space again).

I contacted their customer service repeatedly in order to try and restore access. Unfortunately, it's like talking to a wall. One way I could potentially verify the account would be by providing the transaction ID for at least one of the purchases under the account. Unfortunately, this doesn't work for me, since PayPal will only store such transaction data for three years (or something in that order) and my transaction were before that time. So, I cannot recover them via PayPal and provide them to Steam.

Unfortunately, they refuse to find any other reasonable solution. I e.g. offered to verify that I'm the rightful owner of the associated PayPal account and its log-in e-mail address (which is a different one from the Steam one and still functional), or even by providing ID. However, they didn't respond to that suggestion at all. They just went back to their script and kept droning on about their PayPal transaction IDs. You'd think they could just find a goodwill solution and hand the account over to me, since their server logs should show that nobody else is claiming and using this account. However, they just won't have it. Very frustrating seeing as how they're now holding my purchases hostage and I can't access them. I won't be spending any money with them in the future under these circumstances.

title: Pushed me to piracy to play the games I own
review:
I had a terrible experience with Steam after my account was compromised in October 2024 via a VNC connection while I was away from home. This resulted in a permanent ban from the network, locking me out of thousands of pounds worth of games I've purchased over the years.
I used family sharing to switch between my two accounts – this banned one being my primary with my entire library. The ban not only affects network access, friends, and purchasing but also blocks family sharing and the marketplace. My options were limited: either play on the banned account with zero online features, or abandon my entire game library and start over on my alternate account.
Frustrated, I contacted Steam support three times, but each ticket was closed without any investigation. They simply stated, "It's your account, so it's your responsibility to ensure security." I even reached out directly to Gabe Newell and several Valve executives via email, but received no response whatsoever.
In the end, I've been forced to find alternative ways to access the games I legally own, as Steam has proven unreliable. Anyone claiming Steam provides great recovery support or truly cares about customers is likely exaggerating – I've seen no evidence of that in my case. Avoid putting significant money into Steam if you value account security and customer service.

title: Steam Support mishandled false reporting and refuses to take accountability
review:
On Aug 16, 2025, a user (“balmypup”) posted an unsolicited advertisement under my Action Taimanin screenshot, linking their own screenshot from a completely different game. When I pointed out the lack of relevance, they retaliated by filing a false report. Within hours, Steam Support removed my content and issued me a temporary community ban, all on the same day.

On Aug 17, 2025, after I presented evidence that directly contradicted their claims, Steam admitted the ban was excessive. However, instead of acknowledging their mistake, they insisted the screenshot still needed to be blurred, a backtrack that allowed them to avoid taking accountability.

This shows how Steam’s reporting system can be exploited and how Support mishandles legitimate disputes. Reports from bad actors are actioned instantly, while actual rule violations I flagged were ignored. I’ve since removed all my uploaded content from Steam (screenshots, artworks, videos, and workshop mods - they're hosted on my own site and other platforms anyway) to prevent further false reports, and I won’t be spending another cent on Steam platform from now, and I've been on there for a decade since 2014.

title: Steam’s Security Failed Me, and They Won’t Help Remove My Wrongful Rust Ban
review:
While my Steam client was running at home, I was logged out without doing anything. During that time, a hacker accessed my session and used cheats in Rust for around 42 minutes. I wasn’t even at my PC, yet their actions led to a permanent Rust ban on my account.

Steam hosts and sells Rust but refuses to take any responsibility for this type of session hijacking. When I reported it, they told me the matter was between me, Facepunch, and Easy Anti-Cheat/Epic Games — and they would not help.

This is unacceptable. Steam has the ability to detect unusual logins and session takeovers, yet they failed to protect my account or assist me. Now, a hacker’s actions have permanently damaged my profile, and I have no recourse.

I trusted Steam with my games and account security, but this experience has completely shaken that trust.

title: Been with Steam for over 10 years
review:
Been with Steam for over 10 years, always refunded a product that fails to load or perform properly, or in rare cases even in cases where said product was deemed 'un-refundable'.

The client is stable, feature lists are good (automatic broadcasting options, family share, Steam deck, library listings/filters/sorting).
Integration features for joining games with your friends list etc are just top notch, no footing around.
Having access to games to your games that are no longer available on Steam, is also nice.
"Free Promotions" to try or own games always a good perk.

Earning cards from games is a bit of a gimmick, albeit over the years I have sold my excess and even bought games with those cards via Steam market, so that's a plus, feels kind of like a loyalty program.

The download speeds are good (UK) and max out no problem with multiple locations to choose from... In the event of a net outage, offline mode will let you play games (within the game developers prevue and DRM options) offline.
Major outages seem to be few and far between.

Never had a 'major' issue with them, haven't been hacked, account linking problems, market issues, captcha, Steam Guard/app issues, nada. No VAC bans.

Minor issues;
The newer 'game search' feature on the store is hit and miss, the old search bar filter is much better.
Being showed the same game 10 times on the same store collection page...
Sometimes support doesn't quite 'get it', there's the very rare occasion where I have to re-submit a request to get the help I need - But it's very rare (for me and I rarely need to contact Steam support).

It, for me, is the ultimate gaming library and will probably continue until the day I die. Thanks Steam.

title: My experience with Steam’s app…
review:
My experience with Steam’s app submission process has been quite frustrating. After paying the $100 USD fee to list our app, it was denied without any clear explanation. The game has been running for over two years with no previous issues. Despite fully owning the rights to all content and following every instruction Steam requested, the ban came without clear grounds.

We tried to appeal and ask for a review, but the responses were vague and unhelpful, leaving us unsure about what actually happened. Additionally, no refund or option to resubmit the app was offered, which feels unfair given that we complied with all requirements.

While Steam is a powerful platform, the lack of transparency and support in our case has been disappointing and resulted in a loss of time and money for our team. We hope they improve their review process and communication to avoid situations like this.

title: Wallet funds used without consent — Support ignored clear evidence
review:
I’ve been using Steam for years and trusted it. But after my recent experience, I’ve lost confidence.

On June 25th, 15.59€ was taken from my Steam Wallet via a market purchase I never made. The item remains in my inventory, which makes no sense if someone "hacked" me to steal.

I checked my login activity — no unfamiliar devices. I had already changed my password, logged out of all devices, and linked my phone number.

Yet, another transaction happened the next day (June 26th, 0.04€), proving that unauthorized access still existed — even after account recovery.

Steam Support responded with a generic message, closed my case, and blamed me — even though I never shared my password, never fell for phishing, and even had Steam Guard email verification enabled.

This incident shows that wallet funds can be spent through lingering session tokens or system flaws. And even when you prove it, support refuses to act.

If this happens with a larger balance, you’ll lose it all — and they’ll still deny responsibility.

I’m disappointed that Steam exposes your full email address to anyone who logs in, unlike phone numbers that are partially masked. This is a security risk.

I no longer feel safe making purchases on Steam. They’ve built a system where users are blamed even when clear proof exists.

title: Steam sælger spil, men fralægger sig ansvar som butik / Steam sells games but refuses responsibility as a store
review:
Dansk:
Steam er en platform med utrolig mange og spændende spil, og det er nemt at købe og installere dem.
Men der er desværre en stor bagside, især når noget går galt.

Jeg har oplevet problemer med et spil, jeg har købt via Steam. Spillet har indeholdt fejl og mangler (bl.a. manglende quest-items og grafiske bugs), som har stået uløst i lang tid. På trods af gentagne henvendelser, har Steam nægtet at tage ansvar for produktet, selvom det er Steam, der har solgt det. I stedet henvises man til spiludvikleren, som man så selv skal kæmpe med.

Det virker dybt urimeligt, at Steam kan agere butik og tjene penge på spil, men ikke vil hjælpe, hvis der er fejl eller mangler. Det svarer til at købe et defekt produkt i en fysisk butik og blive bedt om selv at tage kontakt til producenten.

Det gør platformen usikker som forbruger, da man ikke er dækket af reel kundeservice, hvis der opstår problemer.

Som kunde forventer jeg, at den butik jeg handler hos, også tager ansvar. Derfor får Steam her kun 2 stjerner, for brugerflade og spiludvalg, men med et stort minus for deres manglende ansvar og svage support.

English:
Steam offers an impressive variety of exciting games, and it's easy to purchase and install them through the platform.
However, there is a major downside, especially when something goes wrong.
I have experienced ongoing problems with a game I purchased via Steam. The game contains bugs and missing items (such as missing quest objects and graphic glitches) that have gone unresolved for a long time. Despite several attempts to get help, Steam refused to take responsibility for the product, even though they are the seller. Instead, they refer you directly to the game developer and expect you to handle it on your own.
This feels incredibly unfair. If I bought a faulty product in a physical store, I would expect the store to assist me, not to tell me to find the manufacturer and deal with it myself.
This makes Steam an unsafe platform from a consumer perspective, as there's no real customer service support when issues arise.

As a customer, I expect the store I buy from to also take responsibility. Steam gets 2 stars, for its interface and selection, but with a big minus for its lack of accountability and poor support.

title: An unclaimed profile but here goes:
review:
An unclaimed profile but here goes:

A toxic hellhole, filled with the worst humanity has to offer. A community swamped with insults, trolling, award farming, scams, political discourse, etc

Corrupt discussions moderators. They can issue warnings, temp or perm bans on discussions, even if you don't break the rules (really), and these can affect your Steam account and its functionality. Steam Support even agrees that this is the case and states that they discourage moderators from banning as it disrupts discussion and gives the product a bad name. Yet, each community (associated with each game/product) can have it's own set of rules. Moderators however are biased and will ban for negative opinions, even if they're well within Steam's Community Guidelines... end result? Some angry mods can risk the safety and functionality of your Steam account and Valve/Steam support just *shrug*. Like this exploitation is perfectly acceptable and allowed to be tied to a broader account (and no I haven't recently been banned).

Steam moderators in general are also corrupt. If you point out some of Valve/Steam's flaws, i.e. marketplace, awards/jester farming to buy products, monetisation of profiles, widespread scams, the biggest form of DRM of all time (DRM being something people raged about for years but now accept with open arms)... that post will immediately be removed as it is not within Valve's interest. They will cite that it can stir up trouble. It's an opinion and not inflammatory in any way, only unless somebody else sees it that way and reacts negatively against the rules. It's punishment for no crime committed, or anticipating the possibility of one, based on the reception of others. It's ridiculous.

Valve are corrupt. Gabe Newell is a bad person and a greedy sod. And Steam in general has gotta too large and too broad in the digital games distribution world to be trusted in the future.

Shame Randy Pitchford is an awful person too. GOG is the only other option and they're far from perfect, plus the versions of games sold there often have a weak multiplayer experience or one that is completely dead (thanks to Valve's monopoly on PC multiplayer scene and few centralised servers), missing patches/updates, etc.

title: Steam has changed, you can no longer reach a person for support tickets
review: Steam has been generally my go to place to buy games since they launched. I have ~150 games I have purchased on steam over the years. I have always liked steam because I can have most of my games in one place and not have to worry about having to deal with the game launches of multiple companies. The other day I had purchased the base game of "The Division 2" instead of the Ultimate edition by mistake so I went through their refund process and got a refund and then purchased the ultimate edition. So that worked well. I played for several hours and really enjoy the game. Two day later the game goes on sale for half price (the base game was 90% off so that made the entire bundle come out to 50% off). So i go to the refund process and select the "It's now available cheaper" asking for a refund for the price difference and i keep getting a canned response of "you have played for more than 2 hours and are not eligible for a refund" What does the amount of time I have played have to do with the "It's now available cheaper" reason for a refund? If I was just saying that I did not like the game and wanted to return it I would understand the 2 hours playtime limit, but most companies I have delt with have no issues with refunding the difference if you buy something just be fore it goes on sale. My issue with Steam is mainly the fact that you can no longer reach a person for support all you get is canned responses from algorithms. Steam used to be great but this has taught me I should go directly to the game company itself. This makes me sad.

title: Un entorno/comunidad realmente tóxico y…
review:
Un entorno/comunidad realmente tóxico y lleno de ladrones y oportunistas. Como usuario que soy de Steam desde hace más de 10 años he visto como se ha ido convirtiendo en una plataforma de juegos de las más tóxicas que existen. La gente comentan en los foros con lenguaje vulgar y despectivo, con insultos y troleando a todos los que no piensan igual que ellos. Lo peor de todo es que los supuestos moderadores de Steam no hacen nada, tan solo se limitan a borrar mensajes que luego los usuarios vuelven a poner con más inquina aún. Steam se ha convertido en un patio de recreo donde cualquiera puede insultar, coaccionar y ser racista sin ningún tipo de sanción. Por ello no lo recomiendo a día de hoy hasta que no cambie su política de tolerancia contra los insultos y malos modales. Hay otras paltaformas de juegos que son menos tóxicas y que ofrecen los mismos servicios e incluso mejores dadas las actuales circustancias en Steam. Y la novedad ahora es plagiar tus ideas y comentarios. Hay usuarios que se dedican a dar vueltas por los foros para robar ideas y soluciones de otros usuarios y hacerlos pasar como si fueran suyos para ganar puntos...inflingen la ley de propiedad intelectual y es lo más parecido a robar. Steam/Valve no hará nada al respecto, como siempre...mientras hagan dinero todo va de maravilla ¿Verdad? Incluso permitis violencia verbal, robos de propiedad intelectual, etc...

A truly toxic environment/community full of thieves and opportunists. As a Steam user for more than 10 years, I have seen how it has become one of the most toxic gaming platforms that exists. People comment on the forums with vulgar and derogatory language, with insults and trolling everyone who does not think the same as them. The worst of all is that the supposed Steam moderators do nothing, they only limit themselves to deleting messages that users then post again with even more animosity. Steam has become a playground where anyone can insult, coerce, and be racist without any penalty. For this reason, I do not recommend it today until it changes its tolerance policy against insults and bad manners. There are other gaming platforms that are less toxic and that offer the same services and even better given the current circumstances on Steam. And the new thing now is plagiarizing your ideas and comments. There are users who spend their time on forums stealing other users ideas and solutions and passing them off as their own to score points... they're violating intellectual property laws and it's the closest thing to stealing. Steam/Valve won't do anything about it, as usual...as long as they're making money, everything's fine, right? You even allow verbal abuse, intellectual property theft, etc...

title: Other companies, take notes
review:
Take note: Steam REMOVED forced arbitration even though they didn't have to, and nobody was talking about it (The same thing Roku did to hold people's TV hostage) and they just silently removed it without making a big deal, in spite of the removal of forced arbitration being such a net positive for the consumers.

Steam is one of the few platforms left in gaming that cares about the experience, about their customers, and about the big picture (pun not really intended NGL.) They also put the pressure on these devs who will have "open-beta" or preorders that overstay their expected dates, that way the consumer doesn't have to suffer with money in limbo.

While there are things from a business perspective that I disagree with (such as refunds, being a common complaint among others) they haven't been participating in anti-consumer practices the way everybody else in big tech is. They usually win by doing nothing, figuratively, but they're some of the only ones left who understand gamers.

I rarely remember to give out positive reviews, but Steam has been my go-to for years when I want a game. None of the other launchers/marketplaces really match up in my honest opinion. They've also remained very neutral politically, which is what gamers actually want - maybe not the game "journalists," but Steam/Valve also does not try to sway or manipulate my personal narrative. They're just there to hook me up with games I'll like and make money in the process. Doing good and honest business, and I respect that.

This part is just meme-ing and opinion-y, but Gaben is just based. Bless this man, we need more people like him on this sad, modest earth.

12+ years going strong, I am so happy Valve has resisted the dark patterns.

title: Review of Steam’s Rating and Review…
review:
Steam's review system—a feature meant to empower community discourse—falls short by enabling toxicity, discouraging critical feedback, and prioritizing shallow engagement over constructive dialogue.

Key Concerns:

Ad Hominem Attacks Undermine Credibility:

The "Jester" and similar emoji reactions allow users to ridicule reviewers personally rather than engage with their critiques. For example, a negative review of a popular game might be flooded with "Jester" labels without a single counterargument, reducing criticism to a punchline. This fosters a culture where mockery outweighs meaningful debate.

Retaliation Against Critical Voices:

Derogatory reactions risk silencing honesty. Users leaving negative reviews—especially for beloved franchises or early-access games—face disproportionate backlash via emoji spam. This creates a chilling effect, where players may avoid honesty to dodge harassment, skewing the system’s authenticity.

Ethical Risks of Unmoderated Reactions:

By allowing tools that weaponize mockery, Steam tacitly condones mob mentality. For instance, online sources suggest that negative reviews with high "Jester" ratios are either devalued or suppressed, regardless of their validity. Without moderation, this undermines the system’s integrity and Steam’s responsibility as a neutral platform.

title: Steam as a platfom has serious vulnerabilities
review:
I have been in IT and gaming for many years. Since the beginning to be frank. I was there at the very start from the vic 16s to the atari, to comodores to the PC Gaming eras to consoles and so on. I have also worked in the IT corporate world for many years. I have seen many types of systems. I also spend time to study and test systems that I use. One interesting thing about services like Steam is that they mask a lot of problems that exist behind the scenes. At the moment if you have any money in your Steam wallet I suggest that you either spend it or take it out (if you can). There are known scammers on the platform that target accounts with money in their wallets and create fake transactions in order to pocket the money. I know this because it has recently happened to me. The amount was relatively small, but given that the scams occurred when I was not on the platform nor running the application or the games in question are not installed or functioning on my account or device and the transactions seemed in a foreign currency on a day when I was travelling ( so no access to my device on which Steam is installed). Despite all of that. The response from Steam and some community people seems to be that I must have been phished, hacked, hijacked, Malware-ed. A logical person can see that any of those are possible, if one made such a mistake. The frequency and volume of these reported incidents leads a more intelligent mind to start to look at the service provider and those that work and own the system. Do a simple Google search on the subject.. The results are Unbelievable! The also, quite simply, careless support responses and lack of investigation show clearly that they don't care about testing and/or fixing the clear vulnerabilities of their platform.
So folks. By all means use the platform to play games.. But do not leave your card details or any funds in the wallet. YOU HAVE BEEN WARNED!

Was forced to change my username and profile picture because it contained a swastika and the letters "SS" in it. What's funny is that Steam's Rules and Guidelines do not actually prohibit this! Yes, that is correct, having edgy NAZI wording or iconography in a profile is not against their rules or TOS, as Steam absolutely does not mention "Hate Speech" anywhere in their community guidelines rules.

So what was the reason Steam changed my name and profile picture then? I pointed out on their official Reddit page (and was subsequently banned from there too) that this was an obvious double standard. There are 65k players on Steam with the word "Stalin" in their name, and only 500 players with Adolf Hitler. If you search Steam groups for "National Socialism", the only results that come up are actually for pro-communist groups.

So it's clear that Steam and it's moderators, including it's Reddit moderators are biased. They prohibit radical right winged ideologies from being promulgated on their platform while giving the green light to left winged ideologies (which are historically more deadly). Also I am not a bigot or a Neo Nazi. I just wanted to point out my findings based on my personal experience with this platform.

title: Steam Account Hacked - Money Stolen
review:
Some how my account was hacked even though I had Steam Guard activated. This means anybody new trying to log in to my account even if they had the correct password would need a verification code sent to my mobile phone.

The hacker sold a dozen or so items to increase my Steam cash balance. They then purchased items at a much higher value than they were worth.

Queried this with Steam and after over 15 years of loyalty and thousands spent on games I was total sales are final and can't be reversed even though they could clearly see someone had unauthorised access to my account.

Was told in a standard reply that I need to check for malware and change my password. In other words it is all my fault, even though I run paid anti malware and anti virus software and have firewalls on my router and computer.

I checked anyway for unauthorised access on my computer and there was none.

I asked if Steam had a data breach or someone had accessed my account direct through there server. They didn't answer me and instead said there is nothing they can do and closed the message so I couldn't respond again.

Essentially washing there hands. The interesting thing is that Steam acknowledge the account is hacked and know theft has occurred. However they don't just refuse to refund the monies stolen by the hacker, they refuse to refund the commission made on the sales made from the theft.

This to me means they are a very unethical company and purely profit driven with no loyalty to customers.

title: Worst customer support
review:
Terrible Support Experience – No Resolution, Just Avoidance

I’ve been a loyal Steam user for over 10 years, but my recent experience with their support team has been beyond frustrating. My credit card, which I’ve successfully used on Steam and other platforms for years, was suddenly restricted without any prior notification. When I reached out to their support, they claimed the restriction was due to potential disputes or unauthorized activity.

To confirm, I contacted my bank, and they assured me there were no issues on their end. This clearly indicates the problem is with Steam, yet when I explained this to their support team, I was met with indifference.

Instead of addressing the issue, they provided me with completely impractical solutions:

Use retail stores or online shops to buy Steam cards – My country isn’t even listed in their supported stores, leaving me vulnerable to potential scams.
Use a different payment method – I’ve repeatedly explained that I only have one payment method, and I’m not going to incur extra costs to get a new card just for Steam. When I asked if Steam would cover these costs, they ignored the question entirely.
To make matters worse:

They’ve closed 3 of my tickets with an automated response.
I’ve requested to escalate the issue to a supervisor more than three times, yet I’ve been completely ignored.
It’s clear their main goal is to get rid of customers quickly to maintain their support statistics, rather than actually resolving issues. Steam’s lack of accountability, unhelpful responses, and poor customer care have completely eroded my trust in the platform.

If you’re considering using Steam, be aware that if you encounter a payment issue, you’ll be left to deal with it on your own without proper support. Extremely disappointing from such a major company.

title: Steam's customer service and moderation…
review:
Steam's customer service and moderation system have been a point of contention for many users over the years, with countless complaints about slow response times, inconsistent enforcement, and perceived bias in moderation.

**Customer Service:**
Steam’s customer service is often criticized for its slow response times. Users frequently report waiting days or even weeks for a response, only to receive generic, unhelpful replies that fail to address their specific issues. This is particularly frustrating when dealing with issues like account recovery, billing problems, or disputes over purchases. There’s a sense that once you’ve submitted a ticket, your case enters a black hole, and communication becomes a one-way street, with no real resolution in sight. The lack of personalized support, coupled with the overwhelming reliance on automated responses, makes users feel like they’re just another number in a queue.

**Moderation Bias:**
When it comes to Steam’s moderation system, many users feel that there is a noticeable bias in how bans and disciplinary actions are applied. There are frequent reports of users being banned from forums, reviews, and chats without clear reasons or fair warning. In particular, there are accusations that certain groups, opinions, or users with less popular stances are unfairly targeted by moderators, leading to a perceived lack of impartiality. This bias extends to Steam’s review system, where many users claim their honest reviews—particularly negative ones—are removed without proper justification. This stifles open discussion and creates an atmosphere where users feel they can’t freely express their opinions without fear of retribution.

Overall, Steam’s customer service and moderation system often leave much to be desired. While the platform is undeniably a giant in the gaming industry, its customer support and moderation practices can feel outdated, inefficient, and biased. Until these systems are improved, Steam risks alienating a portion of its user base who expect more transparency, fairness, and responsiveness from a platform of its size.

title: Obviously the best place to get games on PC
review:
I see all these extremely low reviews on TrustPilot for what is obviously one of the best companies with the best customer support on PC.

I think the reason the reviews are generally low and spiteful if you read them is because they are not getting refunded. However, if you read Steam's policy before purchasing a game you will note that their strict refund policy allows for refunds so long as you have NOT downloaded the game. They make exceptions to this on the condition that you have played the game for less than 2 hours and are experiencing game breaking problems such as the game not launching. I have been refunded by Steam for about 5 games, and over the years I have bought well over 100 games on Steam.

Perfect service, great community tools!

title: SUPPORT ABUSE
review:
I bought a Steam Deck, which had issues when delivered—okay, it happens. It was partly my fault due to the tricky location, but Steam's support is absolute garbage. If you ask them, 'Hey, who signed for the delivery?' or 'Hey, where did you deliver it?' they won’t tell you anything. They don’t know anything! They just wash their hands of it and say, 'Delivery? We have an email here that says delivered, I don’t know anything else, and I can't tell you anything more. Goodbye, and screw you.' That was my experience with support. And you might say, 'They must have contacted the shipping company or given you the details to contact them.' Nope, they didn’t contact the shipping company, nor did they help me get in touch with that company. The legitimacy is lacking. The same person handled the support the whole time, always replying with CRAP, providing no support whatsoever, and closing tickets WITHOUT ANSWERING ANYTHING. If I ask you for the shipping company's contact info and you answer, 'It was delivered, I don't know anything, screw you,' don't expect anyone to want to leave their money with this company :)

I had already heard about warranty issues because Valve only offers 1 year, while in EUROPE, the law is 2 years. But this? Puff, I spent 500 euros for some guy named Felix to spit in my face? After this terrible treatment, the same guy came back to my ticket and DEMANDED that I return (I don’t want the equipment; I’d never spend another cent on Steam), pay for shipping, and take responsibility in case it gets lost, stolen, broken, etc. If it arrives damaged, it’s my fault, while when they sent the package incorrectly, they took no responsibility. I have the SMS messages with the driver and even the call log with him. You’re a true disgrace, and honestly, the only reason I hesitate to REPORT YOU to consumer protection is the fear you might ban my account, which is worth 2k euros. That’s the value of my account, not counting market items… It’s sad that a company treats USERS WITH SO MANY YEARS on the platform this way. SHAME.

title: Usually using steam is fine but the…
review:
Usually using steam is fine but the problem is that steam app things that it is the most important app on your pc/laptop etc. Steam, when losing connectivity, can cause your game (even if its an offline game) to close without input from you. This is beyond infuriating, not only do you lose progress but then the cloud sync either takes forever or does work which requires you to hard shut down steam and restart it.

Instead, of steam self switching between online and offline modes like Epic does (at least in my experience) after it has been launched the first time in online mode, it disrupts your game play and frankly screws your progress. There are better ways to go about this. Have your app switch between on and offline modes when detecting a change in connectivity, not having it pop to the front of your windows when it finishes launching, not forcing your clients to lose their progress because your app thinks it owns the PC.

title: Two-faced liars and thieves.
review:
Steam does to the videogames and gamers what a wood chipper does to trees.

At best, using Steam is paying full price for temporary usage of games. At worst, which it always is, Steam uses their system explicitly to restrict and double-charge customers at every turn, then they will attempt to steal everything you have purchased, including saved content which they use to market and sell your data.

You'll also see multitudes of complaints and lawsuits against Steam/Valve because they even try to steal products which customers have paid for after their deaths. The entire purpose of which is to coerce friends or family members into paying for the same content yet again. It's very much akin to paying full-price for a movie including DRM's only to have the distribution company (Steam/Valve) come after you later with claims that the games still belong to them.

Steam support also immediately closes thousands of customer service requests for refunds, support, product keys etc. without even reviewing the requests because performing due diligence is just too burdensome for them.

Any gamer with an ounce of respect for the games they play would never use steam or give them a penny. The "discounts" offered through steam are now regularly undercut by better platforms and D2C retailers who will provide you with the game, the key, and DRM for a lower price than you get with Steam. Again, Steam also has a clearly stated objective to steal games, or access to them, back from customers at every opportunity.

title: Steam refuses to take accountability…
review:
Steam refuses to take accountability for selling a known fundamentally flawed game: COD WW2

In the tank mission, the controls become unmanageable, leading to frustration and an almost unwinnable scenario. Several people have registered the same complaint for at least the last 7 years, and nothing has been done to resolve this issue by the COD franchise. I requested a refund for this game for this reason, but because I had played for over 2 hours, Steam policy stated I was not eligible for a refund. However, this mission, at least for me, took more than two hours of gameplay to reach, so by the time I got to this point where the game became unplayable I had already gone past the two-hour mark. So now I have a game I can not play, has no plans to be fixed, and Steam is taking no accountability to refund money. This amounts to deceit and stealing in my opinion. Exceptions must be made and accountability needs to be taken by Steam when they are selling a known flawed product.

title: Steam is one of the best platforms for…
review:
Steam is one of the best platforms for buying games on. it beats its competitors with its wide variety of games available of different genres and generations.

Pro's: Steam sales (such as summer Sale or Christmas sale) have a lot of games on sale for up to 80% off. you can find some gems on the sales like for example Forza horizon 5. I got it on the summer Sale last year for 60% off. they also offer refunds if you don't like the game (or games) you bought including but not limited to DLC's and bundles.

Cons: the website and even the main app can be very unstable and/or laggy. for example when I try to look for skins or stickers on CS2 their website takes forever to load and sometimes even crashes. other times I get disconnected despite having stable and fast WiFi.

If they made their return policy better (giving you a refund directly to your bank rather than steam balance) and their servers more stable, I'd rate it a 5. but since their servers are worse than roblox some how, I rate it 3.

title: Far from perfection because...
review:
Steam platform by Valve Entertainment is very far from perfection for two simple reasons. The first one is their very core EULA rules that imply no rights to users that wants to sell their accounts (illegal) or even just give them away as an inheritance (on the same plain of illegal issues too) to friends, sons, daughters and so on. Second one and even more important is...they have no DRM free (Digital Rights Management) policy. That means, whatever you buy on their platform is not really yours and you have no rights for a digital, standalone copy, of your games/general software sold over there.

They are actually a true anti-God of the collectible era, the analogical era where the importance of a physical copy was relevant to a huge community of impassioned gamers that also believed to the "used in excellent condition" market. Unfortunately, this is partially over.
The only rivals who does DRM free policies are GoG (Galaxy of Gamers) and they are at one step to be the absolute best (they totally lack Linux native support and a Steam Play equivalent through a built-in Proton GE layer).

But Valve, you can certainly improve! Just stop to follow up Gen-Z and Y huge BS (bullsh1t) in a world made of digital illusions and maybe we all will end up differently than a hopeless Roy Batty, like tears in rain.

PS all the silly-billy things I've read about forums and toxicity are irrelevant to me. You can avoid them, simply don't use it. Valve could be more upfront on this topic through an official communication. They can't really defeat social engineering in the net. It's a gray zone with no constitutional rights, just remember that. Don't feed the monsters. Peace & Love.

title: Steam (Valve Corporation) - A Buyer Beware Nightmare: Broken Promises and Unresponsive Support
review:
Steam (Valve Corporation) - A Buyer Beware Nightmare: Broken Promises and Unresponsive Support

My recent experience with Steam, the digital distribution platform owned by Valve Corporation, has left me deeply frustrated and disillusioned. I purchased a game called "King's Call," enticed by the promise of a captivating and polished gaming experience reminiscent of its predecessor, "Rise of Mythos." However, what I encountered was a far cry from what was advertised.

From the moment I launched "King's Call," I was bombarded with a barrage of technical issues. The game was plagued by constant crashes, freezes, and disconnections, making it virtually impossible to progress or enjoy the gameplay. The visuals were blurry, the text was riddled with errors, and the overall interface felt rushed and unpolished. This was not the immersive and captivating experience I had been led to expect.

In a desperate attempt to salvage my investment, I reached out to Steam Support for assistance. However, their response was utterly dismissive and unhelpful. They simply instructed me to troubleshoot the game's technical issues myself, despite the overwhelming evidence from other players indicating that these problems were widespread and systemic.

Seeking a refund for my purchase, I was met with further disappointment. Steam denied my request, claiming that in-game purchases are non-refundable and that the base game being free negates any refund possibility. This response is not only illogical and unfair but also a clear violation of consumer rights. I had spent a significant amount of money on in-game purchases, and Steam's refusal to acknowledge this fact is deeply troubling.

In my frustration, I reached out to the game's developer, King's Call. Their response was equally unhelpful, simply directing me back to Steam for a refund. This circular blame game between Steam and the developer left me feeling trapped and exploited.

This experience has highlighted several critical flaws in Steam's business practices:

* **Misleading marketing and advertising:** Steam's store page for "King's Call" created a false impression of the game's quality and functionality, leading me to make a purchase I would not have otherwise made.
* **Unresponsive and unhelpful customer service:** Steam Support's refusal to acknowledge the widespread technical issues and their dismissive attitude towards my refund request demonstrate a lack of concern for customer satisfaction.
* **Unfair and misleading refund policy:** Steam's policy on in-game purchases and free-to-play games is designed to protect their profits at the expense of consumer rights.

I urge all potential buyers to exercise extreme caution when purchasing games through Steam. Their platform may offer convenience, but it comes at a high cost – the risk of being stuck with a faulty product and no recourse. Save yourself the frustration, disappointment, and financial loss by taking your business elsewhere.

title: Good Storefront
review:
I'm laughing at the majority of negative reviews here who know nothing about how Steam works. In my experience, I've had just about as good an experience on Steam as it can be, as a customer since 2017.
Whenever I've had to contact support (very rarely, admittedly), I've been able to get a response that did assist in questions made.
Even with criticisms that are absolutely justified against this platform (and maybe Valve too), I still find Steam much more trustworthy than other gaming companies.

Revised edit:
I'd also like to add my praise of Steam through additional comments:
Using the platform on a desktop browser and/or while signed out, you don't get spammed with several prompts to install the launcher app, or sign in.
You can browse sites on Steam just as well when signed out (such as store pages, forums, profiles, and inventories).
The website and launcher interfaces get the job done, and as it ain't broke, it ain't tampered with either by Valve.

title: Steam Guard is flawed!
review:
Update:
Steam has reinstated my account and stated any future charge backs could result in a permanent ban. No info on 2FA issues or looking into it and no info on banning the account where the money was sent. Obviously, the seller account that received my money is at least complicit in fraud, if not the fraudster themself.

I have since removed all payment methods from my Steam account and intend to only use Steam gift cards as an extra layer of security. I would highly suggest at a bare minimum not allowing auto payments from Steam through PayPal or storing bank information on your Steam account.

Origin review:
I got hacked due to malware. 8 accounts were having multiple access attempts from across the globe (China, Germany, UK, Vietnam, etc). These accounts were Steam, Amazon, Coinbase, Binance, PayPal, Robinhood, Meta, and Gmail. All but Steam blocked access with Two Factor Authentication, Steam's 2FA didn't even register. $200 charged and sent through their Community Market to another user. I started a support ticket and it was closed without response. I then contacted PayPal and they just about immediately issued my charge back. Now Steam has banned me for issuing a charge back and blames me for not stopping the malware. The malware was caught with live protection, but it took about an hour. I lost $200 in about 5 minutes, though it has been returned. With some searching, it seems I am far from the only one to have their Steam Guard (2FA) bypassed and losing items or money.

title: Steam is the platform that redefined PC…
review: Steam is the platform that redefined PC gaming. Features like Steam Sales, Steam Cloud, automatic updates, the ability to redownload at any time, and integrated chat features made Steam so useful that many people are willing to forego the freedom of DRM-free games in order to have the convenience that Steam gives. I can no longer access my account and have lost hundreds of pounds worth of games, after having password issues.

Instead, they seem to keep adding in a prompt that I should be the original owner of the account in order to regain access... I am.

Their customer service options for being locked out of an account are, frankly, laughable. Looking at the reviews on this page, others seem to have had the same problem. It is probably also worth noting that the customer service team closed my help request/conversation without consulting me on whether I was happy with the help provided or if I had anything else to discuss.

As I only purchased a license to play the games through Steam, rather than the games themselves, I also have no recourse.

Tldr: Steam have taken hundreds of pounds from me and I can't access my account anymore.

title: Steam Promoting Racial Advertising
review:
I am writing regarding Steam's handling of a recent inquiry I made about their promotional strategies, particularly the emphasis on 'Black Gaming of the Month'. My experience with their customer service has raised serious concerns about their transparency and potential racial bias in their advertising choices.

I initially contacted Steam to express my concerns over their seemingly exclusive focus on games related to Black history, while seemingly neglecting the contributions and narratives of other racial and cultural groups within the gaming community. This approach appeared to contradict the principles of diversity and inclusivity, which are vital in the gaming industry.

However, my attempt to engage with Steam on this matter was met with a lack of substantive response. Instead of addressing my concerns directly, Steam suggested that I use their public forums to discuss this issue, a recommendation that I found inappropriate for an internal policy-related matter. Further efforts to seek clarification and a formal response from Steam were abruptly halted when they closed my support topic, without providing any satisfactory explanation or resolution. A practice they never do under other topic to the support group.

This action, and the lack of transparent communication from Steam, has led me to believe that there might be an underlying unwillingness to acknowledge or address potential biases in their promotional strategies. The closure of the support topic, particularly without a proper resolution or acknowledgment of the issue raised, suggests a concerning level of disregard for customer feedback, especially on matters as significant as racial representation and inclusivity.

I wanted to highlight Steam's apparent lack of transparency and possible racial bias in their promotional choices. It is crucial for consumers and the wider public to be aware of how companies handle sensitive issues related to racial representation and customer feedback.

I hope that Steam reevaluates their communication and promotional strategies, ensuring they align with the values of inclusivity and diversity, and that they maintain a transparent and responsive dialogue with their customer base.

title: Steam has been around for decades and…

Steam has been around for decades and is known internationally as the best PC game storefront. They provide an excellent service with a massive variety of games, instant automated no questions asked refunds, and an absolute ton of community features.

The developers (Valve) have also created some of the most popular video game titles including Counterstrike, Dota 2, Portal, Half Life, Left 4 Dead, and Team Fortress 2.

The fact there are so many negative reviews is kind of eye opening to the validity of user based reviews.. The userbase for this software/website is absolutely massive and the majority of users have a positive experience.

title: Steam is A horrible Monopolist who doesn't care about you
review:
Steam is a monopoly in this industry, and that wouldn't be a problem if it weren't for the fact that Steam's customer support is simply terrible, as is the platform itself in many aspects (I won't write about it here because the review will turn out to be very long).

Not to mention that the security in Steam is just pathetic, and they don't do anything about it. I came up with a way to make the protection more reliable overnight after I was scammed, but they release user security updates, if lucky, maybe once every few years. And even then, they deal with the consequences, not the root cause.

As I understand it, they hire people off the street for customer support and pay them pennies because the Steam customer support doesn't give a damn about us.

It seems logical that after someone scam your skins, and Steam bans the person who took them from you, they should return the skins to you, right? But no, Steam couldn't care less, even though you were deceived because Steam can't implement basic protection against trade scams.

I'm sure it's all because a long time ago, people used to trade skins to another account, then contact customer support, and customer support would return the skins like this: they simply duplicated them, and they stayed with the person who supposedly 'stole them' and with you. But then they stopped doing that, and now they won't give you anything back, and they won't unban you no matter what, they just don't care. They might even ban you for frequently contacting customer support...

title: Best Games platform on PC
review:
I've been using Steam since 2011 when my dad set-up an account for me and my brother, fast forward to 2023 and I'm still using it near-daily. The platform is simple to use and has an immense amount of personalisation and customisability for all aspects ranging from player profiles to games library to store preferences. Their Steam Authenticator is one of a kind and extremely easy to secure and protect your account.
People reviewing about being scammed and having their accounts hacked have only themselves to blame due to ignorance of what they are doing. I myself, due to stupidity, have had my account hacked, back before any steam authenticator was setup and therefore can say that it has never been an issue since - my account was returned and restored with the help of customer support.
There is no wonder Steam is the most popular gaming platform on PC due to having the biggest availability of games out of all gaming platforms. The store front is quite simple to use/find the games you love as well as the discovery queue to quickly browse games that are recommended to your gaming tastes.
Can not recommend anything other for those wanting to start out on PC Gaming.
